elements.sent-2411-001: Camera traps, combined with AI, have emerged as a way to achieve automated, scalable biodiversity monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the passive infrared (PIR) sensors that trigger camera traps are poorly suited for detecting small, fast-moving ectotherms such as insects.
elements.sent-2411-003: Insects comprise over half of all animal species and are key components of ecosystems and agriculture.
elements.sent-2411-004: The need for an appropriate and scalable, insect camera trap is critical in the wake of concerning reports of declines in insect populations.
elements.sent-2411-005: This study proposes an alternative to the PIR trigger: ultra-lightweight convolutional neural networks running on low-powered hardware to detect insects in a continuous stream of captured images.
elements.sent-2411-006: We train a suite of models to distinguish insect images from backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our design achieves zero latency between trigger and image capture.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our models are rigorously tested and achieve high accuracy ranging from 91.8\% to 96.4\% AUC on validation data and >87\% AUC on data from distributions unseen during training.
elements.sent-2411-009: The high specificity of our models ensures minimal saving of false positive images, maximising deployment storage efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-010: High recall scores indicate a minimal false negative rate, maximising insect detection.
elements.sent-2411-011: Further analysis using saliency maps shows the learned representation of our models to be robust, with low reliance on spurious background features.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our system is also shown to operate deployed on off-the-shelf, low-powered microcontroller units, consuming a maximum power draw of less than 300mW.
elements.sent-2411-013: This enables longer deployment times using cheap and readily available battery components.
elements.sent-2411-014: Overall we offer a step change in the cost, efficiency and scope of insect monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-015: By solving the challenging trigger problem, we demonstrate a system which can be deployed for far longer than existing designs and budgets power and bandwidth effectively, moving towards a generic camera trap for insects.
elements.sent-2411-016: Artificial Intelligence, Computational Entomology, Conservation Technology, Camera Traps, Insect Declines, TinyML, Biodiversity, Insects.
elements.sent-2411-017: They are also key components of agricultural systems, essential for producing crops and feedstock sustainably, and play a major role in health, as disease vectors affecting humans and animals \citep{insect-disease-vector .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Declines in insect biomass and changes in diversity have received attention in recent years \citep{insect-decline-meta-analysis, death-by-thousand-cuts, 75percent-decline .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Traditional insect monitoring fieldwork methodologies — such as Pan Traps, Pitfalls, and Malaise Traps — scale poorly, due to high labour and time costs \citep{standard-insect-monitoring .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Development of scalable survey techniques is critical for global-scale monitoring of the extent and steepness of declines \citep{scalable-insect-monitoring, standard-insect-monitoring .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Technological advancements in electronics, remote cameras and artificial intelligence offer a route to meet this challenge \citep{synergistic-future, deep-learning-will-transform-entomology .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Already, camera traps have emerged as a popular method to survey populations of large mammals and birds \citep{scaling-camera-traps .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Due to their autonomous nature, camera traps enable 24/7 monitoring across large spatial scales utilising many cameras at a low cost.
elements.sent-2411-024: The large quantities of image/video data produced by camera traps have necessitated the development of AI methods that can automatically process these data by identifying any species present in each image/video \citep{noro-pnas, whytock .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Integration of camera traps and AI, handling data processing, completes an end-to-end scalable architecture suitable for fully automated, and thus globally scalable, biodiversity monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-026: A key feature of camera traps is their trigger mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-027: Triggers, which tell the camera when to capture an image or video, must continuously operate and therefore have the largest impact on system power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-028: The specificity of the trigger (true negative rate), directly impacts the number of erroneous images saved to disk.
elements.sent-2411-029: While its recall (true positive rate) impacts the number of detections.
elements.sent-2411-030: Common fieldwork scenarios where data bandwidth and energy are limited require a low-powered, accurate trigger to enable lengthy deployments.
elements.sent-2411-031: For traditional camera traps, this is facilitated by a passive infrared (PIR) sensor which signals to record data only when sudden changes in surface temperature are detected \citep{pir-misconceptions and draws very little current otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Thus, camera traps can be deployed in remote locations for months at a time, maximising the chance to observe rare or elusive species and minimising human effort.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, PIR triggers are not appropriate for invertebrates \citep{halt-hobbs , due to a lack of body heat to trigger detection.}
elements.sent-2411-034: When PIR sensors have been deployed for insect studies, they are shown to have low specificity \citep{moth-pir and poor recall compared with scheduled image capture \citep{time-lapse-insects .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: For example, \citeauthor{integrated-pest-monitoring-cameras surveys camera systems for integrated pest monitoring.}
elements.sent-2411-037: These systems typically kill species and take time-lapsed footage to attain temporal resolution \citep{integrated-pest-monitoring-cameras .}
elements.sent-2411-038: While successful, the ethics of this alternative are not appropriate to scaling conservation efforts.
elements.sent-2411-039: Other systems based on passive acoustics can be low-powered \citep{buzzometer and prior work has shown that utilising wing-beat harmonics can discern a modest number of species from background noise \citep{9-month-lidar, acoustic-hornets-bees, ivan-mosquitos .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, studies which are able to classify a wider range of species have invariably made use of images input into AI models \citep{stevens2024bioclip, insect-foundation, Hierarchical-insects, august-metadata .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Thus, the use of camera sensors emerges as the preferred method.
elements.sent-2411-042: Non-lethal image-based systems budget power consumption poorly.
elements.sent-2411-043: For example, those filtering on-device, make use of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to improve accuracy but recommend the addition of energy harvesting to enable portability \citep{amt, Insect-detect .}
elements.sent-2411-044: The PICT system has a lower power draw and no such AI capability, but simply records continuously to disk and its deployment length is shown to be limited by storage space \citep{pict .}
elements.sent-2411-045: A recent design using event cameras for triggering is able to reduce power consumption somewhat but requires specialised hardware to do so \citep{gebauer2024towards .}
elements.sent-2411-046: One final challenge of image capture is trigger latency, the time between activation and image capture.
elements.sent-2411-047: This lag is nominally <1s for commercial camera trap systems.
elements.sent-2411-048: Consequently, for fast-moving animals, such as flying insects, small fields of view cannot be tolerated and detection probability is diminished \citep{camera-trap-survey .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Methods which filter a continuous stream of images can be thought of as having zero latency, although these methods invariably require excessive power consumption for processing \citep{motion-vectors .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Here we explore how all aspects of the trigger problem may be tackled using AI executing at the edge.
elements.sent-2411-051: We have designed a novel trigger mechanism for insect capture, recognising (1) that image data are required to achieve high levels of species granularity; (2) that a trigger must be low-powered and accurate, and (3) that hardware platforms should be easily available and low-cost.
elements.sent-2411-052: Our method filters a continuously captured stream of images and searches for those with insect features as learned by a convolutional neural network (CNN).
elements.sent-2411-053: We are able to mitigate power consumption issues by designing our models to suit the latest power-efficient microcontroller hardware.
elements.sent-2411-054: We show how image classifiers can be trained and deployed on low-cost hardware to detect insect instances with high levels of accuracy in real-time and with zero latency.
elements.sent-2411-055: We extensively evaluate our models on validation datasets, out-of-domain examples and via analysis with saliency maps to build trust in the learned representations.
elements.sent-2411-056: We provide an early hardware implementation for an insect camera trap to quantify the power consumption and execution latency of models on-device.
elements.sent-2411-057: Our method is found to be accurate, robust, power-efficient and fast operating on low-cost hardware.
elements.sent-2411-058: The creation of a CNN model for execution on a microcontroller unit (MCU) has many challenges.
elements.sent-2411-059: So-called ``TinyML" algorithms are severely constrained by the computing capability of host devices \citep{tinyml .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The execution memory available on the device limits model size and as MCUs typically have slow clock speeds, the latency for inference is increased, which bottlenecks processing throughput.
elements.sent-2411-061: Initially, object detection architectures seem attractive for our task, as they excel at finding small animals in images \citep{embedded-camera-trap-images .}
elements.sent-2411-062: However, the number of parameters required for object detection often increases tenfold when compared to simple classifiers \citep{mobilenet .}
elements.sent-2411-063: Thus, we take inspiration from the ``Visual Wake Words'' concept \citep{visualwakewords which was shown to reliably identify human-containing images utilising a MobileNet classifier.}
elements.sent-2411-064: We elect to use the MobileNetv2 architecture as it has many built-in efficiencies \citep{mobilenetv2 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: We also simplify the learning problem to its most basic form, two-class (binary) classification, requesting that a single output neuron produces a value of \verb|1.0| for insect presence in the input and \verb|0.0| otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-066: To further save computation, our models use modest input sizes, the smallest of which is 96x96x1, as shown by Table \ref{tab:models .}
elements.sent-2411-067: A limitation of this approach is of course resolution, which makes objects small in the image harder to detect.
elements.sent-2411-068: In practice, this results in a reduced field of view for the deployed system, limiting its ability to detect small objects at a distance.
elements.sent-2411-069: We have also selected modest values for the $\alpha$ parameter, which affects the width of the network and experimented with the removal of the input colour channels which reduces the number of computations per inference.
elements.sent-2411-070: To maximise the efficiency of our models, we use a network compression technique, quantisation \citep{tflite-paper , which reduces the bit-depth of our models such that the data types representing them are more efficiently computed on MCU hardware, typically from 32-bit floating point numbers to 8-bit integers.}
elements.sent-2411-071: This incurs an accuracy penalty and we monitor this throughout the evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-072: Below are details of our method for training and evaluating these models.
elements.sent-2411-073: Specialised training and evaluation datasets for the binary insect classification task are also presented.

elements.sent-2411-075: Selection of an appropriate training dataset was driven by three main factors: (1) the diversity of insect species and inclusion of varied negative background examples, (2) inclusion of images of insects captured in the field to improve model transfer real-world field conditions, (3) the quantity and quality of insect images used.
elements.sent-2411-076: We explore a variety of source datasets for insect classification and detection.
elements.sent-2411-077: The AMI-GBIF dataset, contains over 2.5 million images of 5,364 species \citep{ami-dataset , derived from the GBIF (Global Biodiversity Information Facility).}
elements.sent-2411-078: Indeed the GBIF and other internet-based repositories offer many millions of images across thousands of species.
elements.sent-2411-079: While this coverage appears comprehensive, other datasets contain images of insects as they may be observed in the field \citep{ecostack, Insect-detect, pollinator-detect - this is critical to improve the transferability of learned representations - but they are limited by small sample sizes, featuring only a few species or images with homogenous backgrounds.}
elements.sent-2411-080: To leverage both, we elected to construct a dataset comprising images from the field combined with varied examples from the iNaturalist 2017 challenge dataset \citep{inaturalist .}
elements.sent-2411-081: We select the iNaturalist 2017 dataset due to its broad diversity encompassing 1021 insect species.
elements.sent-2411-082: We elect to use the images annotated with the 125,679 additional insect bounding boxes (hereafter referred to as ``iNat2017-insecta'').
elements.sent-2411-083: This choice was made because a wide range of bounding box sizes - many of which are small - are included, making it ideal for modelling the insect filtering task with the insect at a variety of distances from the camera.
elements.sent-2411-084: As negative samples, we also include the iNaturalist 2017 plantae images as this contains a variety of background foliage, referred to as ``iNat2017-plantae''.
elements.sent-2411-085: Class imbalance is known to bias learned representations, thus, we carefully balance the number of background and foreground images with an equal number of examples from each data source.
elements.sent-2411-086: Images containing insects were assigned the foreground label \verb|1| and \verb|0| (background) otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-087: To add field deployment data, we supplement our dataset with images captured by existing insect camera traps.
elements.sent-2411-088: We use data from \citet{ecostack , hereafter referred to as the ``Ecostack'' dataset and \citet{Insect-detect , hereafter referred to as the ``Insect-Detect'' dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: The number of examples from these datasets is shown in Table \ref{tab:data , these make up a minority portion of the training and validation dataset; they cover 9 and 5 insect classes respectively, labels are also supplied in the bounding box format denoting the spatial location of an insect in each image.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Given the small input sizes required by our models, rescaling large, high-resolution images posed challenges as this often results in a significant loss of subject detail.
elements.sent-2411-091: Datasets, such as Ecostack and Insect-Detect contain high-resolution imagery where the insect subjects occupy only a small portion of the full frame.
elements.sent-2411-092: Full-frame down-scaling strategies for these images are not practical for use with our models.
elements.sent-2411-093: Thus, to leverage their advantages, we preprocess each image by partitioning it into tiles, ensuring that the new resolution is a factor of $2^n$, where $n \in \mathbb{N}$, relative to the original resolution.
elements.sent-2411-094: For label conversion from bounding boxes to binary classification, we assign a label of 1 to a tiled image if it contains a bounding box covering more than 50\% of its area prior to tiling.
elements.sent-2411-095: Images with no overlapping bounding boxes are labelled as 0.
elements.sent-2411-096: In this process, all bounding boxes class labels are treated as the insect class, with the exception of the shadow class in the Insect-Detect dataset, which is treated as background.
elements.sent-2411-097: To achieve balance between the foreground and background classes a number of insect images are chosen and the background tiles are randomly sampled to equal this number or exhaust the number of unique background images.
elements.sent-2411-098: Table \ref{tab:data shows the breakdown of images in our training and validation datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-099: For the validation set, examples from each dataset source's respective validation set are used.
elements.sent-2411-100: A total of 135,451 images containing insects and 134,634 images containing backgrounds results in a train/validation ratio of 84\%/16\%.

elements.sent-2411-101: The training was undertaken using a single Nvidia V100 GPU for each model, utilising computational resources from the JADE2 HPC facility and completed after one week of computation time.
elements.sent-2411-102: Each model was instantiated in the Keras programming environment \citep{keras , using default implementations and hyperparameters listed in Table \ref{tab:models .}}
elements.sent-2411-103: To leverage the advantages of transfer learning, models were pre-trained for 100 epochs on the more diverse iNat2021-mini dataset with randomly initialised weights.
elements.sent-2411-104: Classes are species spread across the Tree of Life and this helps the models learn generic features for natural images.
elements.sent-2411-105: During this stage, a classification head of size 10,000 was added, which refers to the final layer responsible for mapping the learned features to the 10,000 output classes.
elements.sent-2411-106: A categorical cross-entropy loss was employed to facilitate learning.
elements.sent-2411-107: Models used the Adam optimiser \citep{adam with default parameters from the Keras framework (fixed learning rate, $\mu$ = 0.001), and training used a mini-batch size of 64 utilising the NVidia CUDA backend acceleration for TensorFlow.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Each image was resized to fit the input layer using bilinear interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-109: For greyscale images, RGB inputs were converted using the cvtColor function available from the OpenCV library.
elements.sent-2411-110: To help further diversify the data, a binary horizontal flip augmentation was applied with a likelihood of 50\%.
elements.sent-2411-111: Regularisation was added via a dropout layer, with an activation likelihood of 50\%.
elements.sent-2411-112: This was added to the penultimate network layer, before the head \citep{dropout .}
elements.sent-2411-113: After pre-training, the classification head was removed and replaced with a single output neuron for binary classification.
elements.sent-2411-114: The main training comprised a further 10 epochs on our dataset using the same method as above, except, the categorical cross-entropy loss function was replaced with binary cross-entropy.
elements.sent-2411-115: Finally, after training, each model has had its weights quantised such that they can be represented with an \verb|int8| (signed 8-bit integer) datatype.
elements.sent-2411-116: We utilised the TensorFlow Lite for Microcontrollers software package to quantise our models \citep{tflite-micro .}
elements.sent-2411-117: Quantised variants for each model are hereafter referred to as each Q-variant.
elements.sent-2411-118: We conduct a comprehensive evaluation of our models to assess their suitability as insect triggers.
elements.sent-2411-119: Initially, we compute accuracy on the withheld validation set, at a threshold of 50\%, as reported by Keras.
elements.sent-2411-120: While a useful measure, it does not recognise that different use cases may set a threshold for detection based on the user preference for specificity or recall.
elements.sent-2411-121: Thus, we utilise receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves to explore the process of thresholding the ordinal scale output of each model into binary predictions.
elements.sent-2411-122: This allows us to explore the trade-off between recall and specificity at various thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-123: We distil ROC performance to a single value, the area under the curve (AUC) \citep{ROC-curves , appropriately capturing overall performance across all thresholds and allowing models to be easily compared across various configurations and datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-124: A model with a 1.0 AUC value is perfect, one with 0.5 has no discriminative power and one with an AUC value of 0.0 perfectly miss-labels all examples at all thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-125: Using this mode of comparison, we explore how our models adapt to deployment-style datasets which are unseen and seen during training.
elements.sent-2411-126: We compute the AUC for both the Ecostack and Insect-Detect datasets and for two datasets of deployment-style data unseen in training.
elements.sent-2411-127: We choose \citet{pollinator-detect 's dataset of pollinator images (hereafter referred to as Pollinator-Detect) and the camera trap images from the AMI Dataset (hereafter referred to as AMI-CT) \citep{ami-dataset .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: These data are tiled in the same manner outlined in Section \ref{subsec:datasets resulting in a binary classification dataset of field deployment data for a realistic test of performance.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Next, we investigate the legitimacy of the features learned by the model.
elements.sent-2411-130: A common problem in supervised learning is over-fitting to characteristics of the training data which do not generalise to new domains.
elements.sent-2411-131: For example \citeauthor{camera-trap-backgrounds found classification performance to drastically reduce when the same animal is shown with different backgrounds \citep{camera-trap-backgrounds .}}
elements.sent-2411-132: To build trust in learned features, we endeavour to determine the most impactful image regions using saliency maps.
elements.sent-2411-133: Saliency maps graphically show the output gradient with respect to the input image, indicating how sensitive the model's prediction is to changes in each pixel.
elements.sent-2411-134: We make use of the bounding box labels previously discarded in training to quantify the intersection of salient areas and the corresponding area of the image containing an insect.

elements.sent-2411-147: To measure power consumption, we select a deployment platform, compile our models and execute them on the device.
elements.sent-2411-148: MCU code is written to target the ESP32-S3 chipset.
elements.sent-2411-149: We chose this platform as it is low-cost, readily available and supports the ESP-NN neural network acceleration library to speed up inference time, which boosts system throughput.
elements.sent-2411-150: ESP-NN (available at: \url{https://github.com/espressif/esp-nn ) is a C++ library supported by the chip manufacturer, Espressive.}
elements.sent-2411-151: It provides optimised neural network execution functions which are between 3 and 10 times faster than vanilla operations; in our firmware, we use version 1.0.
elements.sent-2411-152: We also use the LILYGO® T-SIMCAM ESP32-S3 Development Board as a target, although similar development boards would suffice.
elements.sent-2411-153: These are available for a retail price of around £20, including an embedded camera, wireless connectivity and the capacity to hold an SD and SIM card.
elements.sent-2411-154: Our testing firmware is very simple, images are captured at a resolution equal to the input size of the network, then, inference is then executed using the TensorFlow Lite Micro runtime and this process is timed.
elements.sent-2411-155: We measure the time taken for inference and average over five inferences for each model.
elements.sent-2411-156: Current consumption is measured using a desk ammeter while idle and while running our programme.
elements.sent-2411-157: The system is powered using a bench power supply set to 3.3V.

elements.sent-2411-179: Our results have revealed our models to be excellent candidates for insect camera trap trigger mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-180: They achieved high accuracy while specificity and recall rates remained robust across output thresholds - even when tested on out-of-domain datasets.
elements.sent-2411-181: Furthermore, saliency map analysis provided insights into the reliability of the learned features, with RGB models consistently focusing on insect-related image regions, reinforcing trust in their generalisation capacity.
elements.sent-2411-182: Our approach also achieved significant reductions in power consumption when deployed on low-powered microcontroller hardware.
elements.sent-2411-183: Our saliency map analysis has given critical insights.
elements.sent-2411-184: Colourblind models have demonstrated a tendency to rely on background information rather than the insect features themselves, as demonstrated by the low proportion of salient pixels in foreground regions.
elements.sent-2411-185: The drop in $\bar{P _t$ is much more serious than a simple drop in accuracy as a brittle learned representation has likely overfit to the training data.}
elements.sent-2411-186: In contrast, the RGB models demonstrated that most salient pixels are located in the foreground, increasing confidence in these models and also serving as a warning that some background correlations have likely been learned throughout the training.
elements.sent-2411-187: Our system's reduced power consumption presents several use cases, primarily in scenarios where energy harvesting is not viable, such as beneath jungle canopies or in other shaded or confined environments such as treetops.
elements.sent-2411-188: The PICT system, a widely cited example, consumes 0.76W during daytime, which allows it to run for 146 hours on a 111 Wh battery.
elements.sent-2411-189: However, with a 64GB SD card, the storage fills up after just three days of recording.
elements.sent-2411-190: The system has a high rate of false positive recordings — during a trial, 1147 hours of footage were recorded, 95\% of which did not capture motion attributed to insect presence, as there was no on-device filtering.
elements.sent-2411-191: In contrast, our system consumes less than half the power of PICT, operating at full frame rate with just 300mW, extending battery life to 370 hours — equivalent to a month's worth of 12-hour daytime periods.
elements.sent-2411-192: Reducing the framerate by half further extends battery life to approximately 600 hours, a massive performance gain.
elements.sent-2411-193: Moreover, the improved specificity of our models will significantly increase the deployment period of an SD card.
elements.sent-2411-194: If just 80\% of false positive frames were removed from PICT, 76\% of trial hours would have been saved.
elements.sent-2411-195: Per SD card this would extend deployment periods to over 12 days.
elements.sent-2411-196: Beyond long-term deployments, our system has potential when used as an external trigger for applications where real-time, low-latency triggers are critical, such as plant-pollinator interaction monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-197: In these cases, where insects remain stationary for a period, our camera trap could act as an external trigger for high-resolution cameras, improving data quality while maintaining low energy consumption overall.
elements.sent-2411-198: This is a step-change in how ectotherms are sensed in the field and may drastically improve poor specificity of pollination ecology studies \citep{moth-pir .}
elements.sent-2411-199: One benefit of edge intelligence is the ability to autonomously make decisions based on what data are interpreted on the fly \citep{standard-ai-insect .}
elements.sent-2411-200: Our method facilitates flexible sampling routines, power consumption could be dynamically budgeted based on environmental conditions or real-time data interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-201: Sampling rates may be increased when insect activity is detected while operating at lower rates during periods of inactivity to conserve energy.
elements.sent-2411-202: While promising, this method has several limitations that can be explored in future work.
elements.sent-2411-203: Power consumption is markedly lower than similar systems, but can be reduced much further in the idle state by utilising further firmware engineering aspects such as utilising MCU sleep modes which may drastically reduce power consumption between model inferences or during system down-times.
elements.sent-2411-204: Our models have also encountered challenges in generalising to the challenging out-of-domain Pollinator-Detect dataset, suggesting they require refinements for some downstream use cases.
elements.sent-2411-205: This could be addressed in the selection of training data, as more field datasets become available, domain shift size may diminish, allowing higher accuracy scores.
elements.sent-2411-206: The method we have shown allows further training data to be integrated easily, improving flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-207: The low input resolution of our models limits their field of view, this constraint was required by the compute capability of MCU hardware.
elements.sent-2411-208: Recent innovations in lightweight, shallow convolutional networks may provide larger input sizes within the same compute budget \citep{fomo .}
elements.sent-2411-209: This may allow for higher-resolution imagery to be processed improving detection of small insects in-frame.
elements.sent-2411-210: The framerate throughput of our MCU deployment example is considerably lower than competing systems which record video continuously.
elements.sent-2411-211: This constraint is required by the execution latency of our models but can be corrected in several ways.
elements.sent-2411-212: Further, model latency could be reduced by using additional compression methods such as network pruning \citep{pruning .}
elements.sent-2411-213: Finally, while in this work we focus on addressing insect monitoring, these concepts could be applied to the retrieval of animal images more generally.
elements.sent-2411-214: Our method, with zero latency, and high accuracy provides new directions.
elements.sent-2411-215: This method as a replacement for, or working alongside, PIR triggers in traditional camera traps may help to address low recall of fast-moving small animals and address the large number of false positive triggered images.
elements.sent-2411-216: Using the latest innovations in MCU hardware and lightweight supervised learning, we have shown how insect monitoring can be made scalable by solving the challenging trigger problem.
elements.sent-2411-217: Through extensive analysis, we have established trust in our methods, proving that our models are well-suited for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-218: Our early hardware implementation is also novel and saves energy and cost using readily available components.
elements.sent-2411-219: This method has the potential to transform non-invasive insect monitoring in the future.
elements.sent-2411-220: Our solution is scalable, providing a step-change in capability, allowing systems to be deployed in greater quantity, improving sampling density and scale without the need for excessive manual labour.
elements.sent-2411-221: Such tools are essential for the timely assessment of biodiversity trends in the wake of current environmental uncertainties.. \bibliographystyle{unsrtnat.}
elements.sent-2411-222: However, the passive infrared (PIR) sensors that typically trigger camera traps are poorly suited for detecting small, fast-moving ectotherms such as insects.
elements.sent-2411-223: The need for an appropriate and scalable insect camera trap is critical in the wake of concerning reports of declines in insect populations.
elements.sent-2411-224: Our models are rigorously tested and achieve high accuracy ranging from 91.8\% to 96.4\% AUROC on test data and 58.8\% to 87.2\% AUROC on field data from distributions unseen during training.
elements.sent-2411-225: Our method is also shown to operate deployed on off-the-shelf, low-powered microcontroller units, consuming a maximum power draw of less than 300mW.
elements.sent-2411-226: This paves the way for scalable systems with longer deployment times.
elements.sent-2411-227: Overall, we fully define the properties of a successful trigger for camera traps and show how lightweight AI models, made bespoke for efficient hardware can be realised with a specific focus on insect ectotherms.
elements.sent-2411-228: We provide these models to the community alongside a complete codebase for future modifications and we demonstrate how they can be deployed on an example ESP32-S3 microcontroller platform.
elements.sent-2411-229: This step potentiates a major advancement in ectotherm camera traps and insect monitoring.

elements.sent-2411-233: Declines in insect biomass and diversity changes have received attention in recent years \citep{insect-decline-meta-analysis, death-by-thousand-cuts, 75percent-decline .}
elements.sent-2411-234: Camera traps have emerged as a popular method to survey populations of large mammals and birds \citep{scaling-camera-traps .}
elements.sent-2411-235: Due to their autonomous nature, they enable continuous (24/7) monitoring across large spatial scales utilising many cameras at low cost.
elements.sent-2411-236: The large quantities of image/video data produced by camera traps have necessitated the development of AI methods that can automatically identify species present in each image/video \citep{noro-pnas, whytock .}
elements.sent-2411-237: Integration of camera traps and AI, handling data processing, completes an end-to-end architecture suitable for fully automated, and thus globally scalable biodiversity monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-238: However, due to remote deployments where access to energy sources and data transfer capabilities are limited, this scalability is constrained by two critical factors: power consumption and data volume.
elements.sent-2411-239: Integral to these constraints is the trigger mechanism, a signal that determines when a camera trap captures images/video.
elements.sent-2411-240: Designing an effective trigger is challenging as it must optimise each of the following aspects:.
elements.sent-2411-241: Storage Efficiency: Since a trigger always signals data capture, it governs the quantity of data which a system gathers, directly impacting deployment time as full SD cards require manual changing.
elements.sent-2411-242: Responsiveness: Since a trigger is the only signal for data capture, it must consistently activate when the target is present.
elements.sent-2411-243: A trigger which is always activating is maximally responsive but will also capture the maximum amount of false positives (“blank” images/video), compromising storage efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-244: Latency: We define this as the time taken between trigger activation and image capture.
elements.sent-2411-245: Minimal latency is required to ensure that fast-moving animals are still within camera field-of-view (FOV) when images/video are captured.
elements.sent-2411-246: A trigger system with excellent responsiveness can still save blanks if latency is high.
elements.sent-2411-247: Energy: Triggers are in constant operation and thus can have the largest impact on system power consumption, affecting the scalability of camera trapping surveys via battery life depletion and expense of additional energy systems (e.g. larger batteries or solar panels).
elements.sent-2411-248: Traditional camera traps have typically balanced these requirements via the use of passive infrared (PIR) sensors which signal to record data only when sudden changes in surface temperature are detected \citep{pir-misconceptions and consume negligible power otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-249: They can be deployed in remote locations for months at a time, maximising the chance to observe rare or elusive species and minimising human effort.
elements.sent-2411-250: However, PIR triggers are not appropriate for invertebrates \citep{halt-hobbs , due to a lack of detectable body heat.}
elements.sent-2411-251: When PIR-triggered camera traps have been deployed for insect monitoring trials, they suffer from poor storage efficiency, resulting in many ``blank'' images/videos \citep{moth-pir and poor responsiveness compared with scheduled image capture \citep{time-lapse-insects .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: Consequently, the design of insect-focussed monitoring devices cannot rely on a conventional triggering method.
elements.sent-2411-253: There are some alternatives which negate the requirement for a trigger.
elements.sent-2411-254: These systems typically attract species (e.g. pheromone lures) and capture them fatally (e.g. sticky paper or liquid).
elements.sent-2411-255: Often cameras then take time-lapsed footage giving temporal resolution \citep{integrated-pest-monitoring-cameras .}
elements.sent-2411-256: Whilst no trigger is required, the ethics of this method are not appropriate to scaling conservation efforts \citep{ethics-lethal .}
elements.sent-2411-257: Conversely, time-lapsed footage without lethal means disregards storage efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-258: Event-camera based systems offer low power consumption \citep{gebauer2024towards , but these are currently prohibited by cost.}
elements.sent-2411-259: Options based only on passive acoustics can also operate with low power consumption \citep{buzzometer and prior work has shown that wing-beat harmonics can discern a modest number of species from background noise \citep{9-month-lidar .}}
elements.sent-2411-260: While audio provides a data-efficient, low-power route, image data is crucial for developing and applying existing AI models with broad taxonomic coverage \citep{stevens2024bioclip, insect-foundation, Hierarchical-insects, august-metadata .}
elements.sent-2411-261: Thus, the search for an effective insect camera-based trap remains the most active.
elements.sent-2411-262: The plant–insect interactions camera trap (PICT), without a trigger mechanism, continuously records video to disk and can achieve low power draw utilising optimisations provided by the Raspberry Pi system \citep{pict , it can run for a few days before storage is depleted.}
elements.sent-2411-263: More recent alternatives opt to integrate AI capabilities into devices and perform analysis in the field to reduce the empty image storage burden \citep{Insect-detect, darras, amt, bjerge-jetson .}
elements.sent-2411-264: Such AI pipelines developed for insect camera traps have followed trends in traditional camera trap AI development; object detection models initially find areas of the image which contain insects and classifiers determine species labels.
elements.sent-2411-265: A problem with this paradigm is that the compute power required for these object detectors is more than the PICT; with consequent power consumption on the order of several 1.3-12.5W for each system (further details in Table \ref{tab:look-at-me ).}
elements.sent-2411-266: Their advantage is continuous recording with the recommended addition of continuous energy via solar panels.
elements.sent-2411-267: While effective, this makes systems more expensive due to the cost (e.g. of charge control boards and solar panels) and less portable due to deployment requiring continuous strong sunlight for energy harvesting.
elements.sent-2411-268: A trigger to accurately determine data volume only at low energy cost remains a critical ingredient.
elements.sent-2411-269: Here, we explore how all aspects of the trigger problem may be tackled using image-based ultra-lightweight AI designed to execute on microcontroller units (MCUs) at the edge, meaning deployed on platforms in the field where power and data volume limitations are significant.
elements.sent-2411-270: Our design recognises (1) that image data are required to achieve high levels of species granularity; (2) that a trigger must be storage efficient, responsive, and operate with minimal latency and (3) that the trigger should suit hardware platforms which are low-powered, in this case we target MCUs.
elements.sent-2411-271: Our trigger allows a continuously captured stream of images to be processed and searches for those with insect features as learned by a convolutional neural network (CNN).
elements.sent-2411-272: Via carefully constructed accuracy measures, we measure the storage efficiency and responsiveness of the trigger.
elements.sent-2411-273: We also show the trigger operating on the latest power-efficient microcontroller hardware to measure its power consumption and quantify quantisation error.
elements.sent-2411-274: Further, to build trust in our method, we extensively evaluate our models on test datasets, example images from field trials and via analysis with saliency maps to explain the representations learned by the models.
elements.sent-2411-275: The result is a suite of AI models with especially low computational burden, high accuracy and trustworthiness.
elements.sent-2411-276: We provide all model weights, extensive software documentation and guidance for others to develop their own variations for ectotherm-focussed camera traps.
elements.sent-2411-277: Creating a CNN model for execution on MCUs has many challenges as these so-called ``TinyML" algorithms are severely constrained by the computing capabilities of host devices \citep{tinyml .}
elements.sent-2411-278: This is because (1) the execution memory available on the device limits the number of model parameters which can be used and (2) low clock speeds of MCUs increase the computation time for model inference.
elements.sent-2411-279: Initially, object detection architectures seem attractive; they excel at finding small animals in images \citep{embedded-camera-trap-images and have been widely used for camera trapping workflows.}
elements.sent-2411-280: However, object detection models require a significant number of parameters, regularly increasing tenfold or more when compared with more simple classifier models \citep{mobilenet , making them ill-suited to microcontroller deployment.}
elements.sent-2411-281: Thus, we take inspiration from the ``Visual Wake Words'' concept \citep{visualwakewords which reliably identifies human-containing images using a MobileNet classifier with far fewer parameters than MobileNet object detectors.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Once a model is found, it can be integrated into microcontroller firmware to continuously capture images and determine if they contain an insect.
elements.sent-2411-283: This workflow, shown by Figure \ref{fig:pipeline , eliminates latency between trigger and image capture by making decisions retrospectively, eliminating the risk of blank images caused by slow trigger speeds.}
elements.sent-2411-284: The original image can also be saved at full resolution for downstream processing after a decision is made.
elements.sent-2411-285: The rate at which image capture can be achieved is determined by the execution latency of the model, faster models ensure higher detection probability of fast animals moving through the camera field-of-view (FOV).
elements.figure-2411-010: [H] \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figs/Presentation4-2-cropped.pdf} \caption{Software pipeline showcasing how the trigger (CNN, binary classifier) can be built into programmes on deployed devices. Initially, images are captured and a preprocessed variant of the image is fed into the model for prediction. Logic thresholds the prediction to warrant saving the original image, this loop then repeats.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-286: Following the ethos efficient models,we use the MobileNetv2 architecture, which incorporates features that reduce computation, such as depthwise separable convolutions, residual bottleneck layers, and the ReLU6 activation function, which is tolerant to low precision \citep{mobilenetv2 .}
elements.sent-2411-287: MobileNetv2 also includes an $\alpha$ parameter that adjusts the network's width, enabling a trade-off between model complexity and computational efficiency; $\alpha$ values below 1.0 reduce the network's width and computational burden \citep{mobilenet .}
elements.sent-2411-288: For a full description of the MobileNetv2 architecture, we refer the reader to the respective MobileNet and MobileNetv2 papers.
elements.sent-2411-289: We selected modest values $\alpha$ values of 0.1 and 0.35 as part of a heuristic approach to explore suitable configurations for deployment on resource-constrained microcontroller hardware.
elements.sent-2411-290: We experimented with removing input colour channels to reduce computations per inference.
elements.sent-2411-291: We also simplify the learning problem to a basic form of binary classification, using a single output neuron to indicate insect presence (\verb|1.0|) or absence (\verb|0.0|) in the input.
elements.sent-2411-292: To further save computation, our models use modest input sizes, the smallest of which is 96x96x1 and the largest of which is 120x160x3.
elements.sent-2411-293: This results in a small number of parameters for each model, as shown by Table \ref{tab:models .}
elements.sent-2411-294: Using a smaller input size for the models has several down-stream implications: (1) image down-scaling may be required to input an image to a model, potentially reducing detection probability for small objects in high-resolution images with a wide FOV, as is common for insect camera trap field datasets \citep{Insect-detect, ecostack, pollinator-detect, ami-dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-295: When resized, these objects lose excessive detail, making them difficult to discern (Figure S3).
elements.sent-2411-296: To address this, we propose a “tiling” approach, which allows the model to repeatedly process smaller sections of each image at higher resolution.
elements.sent-2411-297: A detailed description of this method is provided in Section \ref{sec:tiling .}
elements.sent-2411-298: This approach is suitable for scenarios like pollination studies or lure-based setups, where insects are expected in a specific area.
elements.sent-2411-299: Positioning the camera closer reduces the field of view, aligning with the model's capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-300: Although smaller fields of view limit the sampling area, this can be balanced by scalability, such as longer deployments or deploying more systems \citep{darras .}
elements.sent-2411-301: One final step we have taken to ensure these models can run on MCU platforms is using quantisation.
elements.sent-2411-302: This technique reduces the precision of a neural network’s internal data (i.e. weights and activations) into representations requiring a lower number of computational bits to represent them.
elements.sent-2411-303: Quantisation is commonly used for neural network deployments to microcontrollers as their computation architectures are typically optimised for operations on 8-bit integers.
elements.sent-2411-304: There are many flavours of quantisation, we utilise post-training quantisation, which turns our floating point weights into 8-bit signed integers.
elements.sent-2411-305: This is performed using the tools provided by TensorFlow, and follows the protocol outlined by \cite{tflite-paper .}
elements.sent-2411-306: Critically, we note that the compression of the model data incurs an accuracy penalty and we monitor this throughout our evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-307: Below are details of our method for training and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-308: Specialised datasets for the binary insect classification task are also presented.

Models were instantiated with the Adam optimiser \citep{adam}, a fixed learning rate, $\mu$ = 0.001.} \label{tab:models}
elements.sent-2411-309: Selection of an appropriate training dataset was driven by three main factors: (1) the diversity of insect species and inclusion of varied background examples, (2) inclusion insect images captured in the field to improve model transfer to real-world field conditions, (3) the quantity and quality of insect images.
elements.sent-2411-310: The AMI-GBIF dataset contains over 2.5 million images of 5,364 species \citep{ami-dataset , it depicts insect images from citizen science platforms and from museum specimens.}
elements.sent-2411-311: Thus, the context of insects is varied, with some having diverse backgrounds in-situ and others with homogenous backgrounds such as backboards from museum collections.
elements.sent-2411-312: Other datasets contain insects images observed by automated insect camera traps \citep{ecostack, Insect-detect, pollinator-detect .}
elements.sent-2411-313: Leveraging these is judged critical to improve the transferability of learned representations to automated field scenarios but they are limited by small sample sizes featuring only a few species and images with homogenous backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-314: We elected to construct a dataset comprising images from the field combined with varied examples from the iNaturalist 2017 challenge dataset \citep{inaturalist .}
elements.sent-2411-315: We use those annotated with the 125,679 additional bounding boxes (``iNat2017-insecta'').
elements.sent-2411-316: This dataset includes a wide range of bounding box sizes, many small, this is important for training the model to recognise different subject sizes.
elements.sent-2411-317: As negative examples, we also include the iNaturalist 2017 plantae images as these contain a variety of background foliage (``iNat2017-plantae'').
elements.sent-2411-318: Class imbalance is known to bias learned representations, so we carefully balance the number of background and insect images with an equal number of examples from each source.
elements.sent-2411-319: Images containing insects were assigned the label \verb|1| and \verb|0| otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-321: These make up a minority portion of the training and test dataset and they cover 9 and 5 insect classes respectively; labels are also supplied in the bounding box format denoting the spatial location of an insect in each image.
elements.sent-2411-322: The overall training and test data mix is shown by Table \ref{tab:data .}
elements.sent-2411-323: Our models require small input sizes, making resizing a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-324: Ecostack and Insect-Detect feature high resolution images where insects occupy only a small portion of the frame, thus, simply downscaling them is impractical for our models as important subject details are lost (Figure S3).
elements.sent-2411-325: To address this, we preprocess these images by splitting them into a grid of "tiles", similar to a jigsaw puzzle, ensuring the tile's size is a factor of $2^n$, relative to the original image size (Figures S1 and S2 show tile examples).
elements.sent-2411-326: To convert their labels from bounding boxes to binary classification, we assign a label of 1 to a tiled image if it contains a bounding box with more than 50\% of its area prior to tiling within the tiled image.
elements.sent-2411-327: Images containing overlapping bounding boxes covering less than 50\% of the bounding box are not included in our dataset.
elements.sent-2411-328: Discarding these images encourages the model to focus on clear, complete insect examples, avoiding confusion from challenging cases with minimal insect content.
elements.sent-2411-329: A disadvantage of this approach is that the training data are made less various, this may reduce generalisation.
elements.sent-2411-330: A 50\% threshold is selected to balance these trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-331: All bounding box class labels are treated as insect instances, except for the shadow class in Insect-Detect, which is treated as background.
elements.sent-2411-332: To balance the insect and background images, the required number of insect images (Table \ref{tab:data ) are selected, and background tiles are randomly sampled to match this number or exhaust those available.}

elements.sent-2411-333: The training was undertaken using a single NVIDIA V100 GPU for each model, utilising computational resources from the JADE2 HPC facility and completed after one week of computation time.
elements.sent-2411-334: Transfer learning accelerates model training and improves performance by leveraging knowledge from related tasks; for example, a model pre-trained on human images may gain better performance from further training to distinguish individuals versus training from a randomised start point.
elements.sent-2411-335: To leverage these advantages, models were first pre-trained for 100 epochs on the iNat2021-mini dataset with randomly initialised weights.
elements.sent-2411-336: Categorical cross-entropy loss was used to facilitate learning, with training conducted using a mini-batch size of 64 and NVIDIA CUDA acceleration for TensorFlow.
elements.sent-2411-337: As is common in deep learning, we apply image augmentations to further diversify our training data.
elements.sent-2411-338: Augmentations are (often) basic operations which modify images to provide new examples to the model.
elements.sent-2411-339: We have applied an operation which flips images around the vertical axes and executes with a likelihood of 50\%; the training data are diversified with mirror images.
elements.sent-2411-340: Regularisation is another technique which improves model performance by artificially making training more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-341: We added this via a dropout layer, which is a component that randomly deactivates neuronal connections during training, with a probability of 50\% \citep{dropout .}
elements.sent-2411-342: Our dropout layer was added to the penultimate network layer, before the head.
elements.sent-2411-343: After pre-training, the classification head was replaced with a single output neuron for binary classification.
elements.sent-2411-344: The main training comprised a further 10 epochs on our dataset, using the same method as above and replacing the categorical cross-entropy loss function with binary cross-entropy.
elements.sent-2411-345: After training, each model has had its weights quantised such that they can be represented with an \verb|int8| (signed 8-bit integer).
elements.sent-2411-346: We conduct a comprehensive evaluation of our models to assess their suitability as camera trap triggers, focussing on the characteristics outlined in the Introduction.
elements.sent-2411-347: First, model accuracy metrics are used to evaluate the storage governance and trigger responsiveness, then saliency maps are used to evaluate the model's insights, finally an example MCU deployment quantifies power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-348: There are many correctness metrics for a binary predictive test, each uses a combination of the following elements: (1) True Positives (TP), the number of positive and correct predictions; (2) True Negatives (TN), the number of negative and correct predictions; (3) False Positives (FP), the number of positive and incorrect predictions; and (4) False Negatives (FN), the number of negative and incorrect predictions.
elements.sent-2411-349: A commonly used metric is accuracy, which is the fraction of correct predictions (TP + TN) out of all predictions (TP + TN + FP + FN), this measure gives a good indication of overall performance, but, it does not differentiate the impact of TP versus TN cases.
elements.sent-2411-350: Further metrics include the precision, which is the number TP out of all positive predictions (TP + FP) - this evaluates the correctness of positive results only.
elements.sent-2411-351: Another is recall which evaluates TP as a fraction of the total positive examples that were available (TP + FN) - this evaluates the ability to find all positive examples and thus models the trigger responsiveness.
elements.sent-2411-352: The F1 score is an average of precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-353: While useful, this measure omits the value of TN.
elements.sent-2411-354: This is significant, as negatives may be very high in a scenario where insects visits are rare, thus the ability to identify negative cases is important to understand the trigger's storage governance \citep{motion-vectors .}
elements.sent-2411-355: To address this, we also consider specificity which can be thought of as the recall of negatives; it is the portion of TN out of all available negatives (TN + FP).
elements.sent-2411-356: A further challenge is considering where to place the threshold at the model output before a positive prediction can be declared.
elements.sent-2411-357: Different ecological scenarios may have different requirements.
elements.sent-2411-358: In a situation where one cannot afford to miss an observation, this threshold may be set low (i.e. 0.1) to ensure the output is more readily positive at the expense of a greater number of FP.
elements.sent-2411-359: On the contrary, in a scenario where storage governance is paramount, the threshold may be set higher (i.e. 0.9) at the expense of FN.
elements.sent-2411-360: As these use-cases compete, we summarise them using a receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve, which plots the recall and specificity at various output thresholds \citep{ROC-curves .}
elements.sent-2411-361: We also distill this curve to a single value by computing the area under the ROC curve (AUROC), appropriately capturing overall performance across all thresholds and allowing models to be easily compared across various configurations and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-362: A model with a 1.0 AUROC value is perfect, one with 0.5 has no discriminative power and one with an AUROC value of 0.0 perfectly miss-labels all examples at all thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-363: We recognise that many other works in this area investigate accuracy, precision, and F1 scores so we also provide these on the withheld test set at an output threshold of 50\%.
elements.sent-2411-364: Using this comparison method, also compute the AUROC for both Ecostack and Insect-Detect and for two field insect camera trap datasets unseen in training.
elements.sent-2411-365: We choose \citet{pollinator-detect 's dataset of pollinator images (Pollinator-Detect) and the camera trap images from the AMI Dataset (AMI-CT) \citep{ami-dataset .}}
elements.sent-2411-366: These data are tiled in the same manner as described in Section \ref{sec:tiling , resulting in a further challenging test from realistic unseen field data.}
elements.sent-2411-367: A common problem in supervised learning is overfitting to training data characteristics that fail to generalise in new domains.
elements.sent-2411-368: For example, \citeauthor{camera-trap-backgrounds found classification performance to drastically reduce when the same animal is shown with different backgrounds \citep{camera-trap-backgrounds .}}
elements.sent-2411-369: To build trust in the learnt features, we endeavour to determine the most impactful image regions using saliency maps.
elements.sent-2411-370: Saliency maps graphically show the output gradient with respect to the input image, indicating how sensitive the model's prediction is to changes within pixel regions.
elements.sent-2411-371: While there are many ways to create saliency maps \citep{molnar2022 , we chose the "Vanilla Gradient" method because it is the simplest.}
elements.sent-2411-372: This method calculates how each region in an image influences the model's prediction by measuring how a small change in each pixel affects the output value.
elements.sent-2411-373: The result is a heatmap, the same size as the input image, where brighter areas show the pixels with steepest output gradient.
elements.sent-2411-374: We implement a saliency map analysis pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-375: This process, visually shown in Figure \ref{fig:sal-workflow , highlights how we measure the overlap between the model's most influential regions and the insect locations annotated by bounding boxes.}
elements.sent-2411-376: Since our model has a single output neuron representing both classes, we focus only on positive gradients - those that signal the presence of insects (positive outputs).
elements.sent-2411-377: This is shown in Equation \ref{eqn1 , where $S$ is the saliency map, $I$ is the input image and $y$ is the output prediction. \begin{equation}\label{eqn1} S_k = \max\left(0, \frac{\partial y}{\partial I_k}\right)\end{equation} For each image, labelled by the index $k$, the resulting saliency map is normalised so that its values fall in range between 0 and 1.}
elements.sent-2411-378: We then binarise these maps at different thresholds, $t$ (ranging from 0 to 1), this turns them into black-and-white images where only the most important pixels remain.
elements.sent-2411-379: This process is shown mathematically by Equation \ref{eqn2 : \begin{equation}\label{eqn2} S_{k,t} = \begin{cases} 1, \text{if } S_k \geq t, 0, \text{if } S_k < t \end{cases}\end{equation} Next, to evaluate how well the saliency maps highlight insect image regions, we calculate $P^t_k$ as the proportion of pixels within a thresholded image, $S_{i, j ^{k, t}$, which lie within the range of any insect bounding boxes for that image, i.e. the proportion of salient pixels which are in the correct area.}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eqn2} S_{k,t} =  1, if  S_k \geq t, 0, if  S_k < t \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-380: To gain an average performance across all images, we compute $\bar{P ^t$ as the average $P^t_k$ over all $k$ as shown by Equation \ref{eqn3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-381: We repeat this for all models and $\bar{P}$ is plotted over all values of $t$ to provide a visualisation of model performance.
elements.sent-2411-382: As the value of $t$ rises, $\bar{P}$ is expected to rise, indicating that the most salient pixels consistently lie within the image portion containing an insect, and not background regions which may be misleading.
elements.sent-2411-383: We plot $\bar{P ^t$ for our test data, producing a robust analysis of the learned “insect” representation. \begin{equation}\label{eqn3} \bar{P}_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_{k,t}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-384: Finally, minimisation of power consumption has been a key driver for the design decisions taken throughout this work and thus forms the final component of our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-385: Firmware is written to target the ESP32-S3 chipset.
elements.sent-2411-386: We chose this platform as it is low-cost, readily available and supports the ESP-NN neural network acceleration library to speed up inference time.
elements.sent-2411-387: It provides optimised neural network execution functions which are between 3 and 10 times faster than equivalent operations without using the library; in our firmware, we use version 1.0.
elements.sent-2411-388: We also use the LILYGO® T-SIMCAM ESP32-S3 Development Board as a target.
elements.sent-2411-389: Some alternatives could be boards which use chipsets that have support TensorFlow Lite builds (a list is available online: \url{https://ai.google.dev/edge/litert/microcontrollers/overview ), although, this paper does not intend to make specific hardware recommendations.}
elements.sent-2411-390: Our testing firmware is very simple, images are captured at a resolution equal to the input size of the network, then, inference is then executed using the TensorFlow Lite Micro runtime \citep{tflite-micro and this process is timed.}
elements.sent-2411-391: The test accuracy, precision, F1 scores and AUROC values for each model variant are summarised in Table \ref{tab:results , and ROC curves are presented in Figure \ref{fig:roc-curves .}}
elements.sent-2411-392: Further F1 and precision scores for all datasets are available in the Supplementary Material (Table S1).
elements.sent-2411-393: Across all models, test accuracy ranges from 0.836 to 0.904, precision scores from 0.873 to 0.917 and F1 scores from 0.823 to 0.898 indicating strong performance.
elements.sent-2411-394: This trend is consistent across output thresholds, with test AUROC ranging from 0.918 to 0.964.
elements.sent-2411-395: We report quantised model accuracy figures in Table S1 and found that quantisation did not significantly affect these measures.
elements.sent-2411-396: The colourblind models generally underperform compared to models utilising RGB inputs.
elements.sent-2411-397: This is particularly evident for the more challenging Pollinator-Detect dataset, where AUROC values fall considerably.

elements.sent-2411-399: Performance on Insect-Detect and Ecostack is strong and consistently outperforms the test set.
elements.sent-2411-400: This result is not surprising as examples from these datasets are included in the training mix (Table \ref{tab:data ) and have less diverse backgrounds than those in the iNat2017 subset.}
elements.sent-2411-401: The results indicate that our models demonstrate high discriminative power for unseen data with insects on simple, uncluttered backgrounds and strong overall performance on data from the same distribution as the training source.

elements.sent-2411-406: Our methodology balances the complex requirements of an ideal camera trap trigger, offering a novel approach to detecting ectotherms in camera trap systems while adhering to the data volume and power constraints of remote environments.
elements.sent-2411-407: We have diverged from the traditional approach of PIR sensors and the popular insect camera trap approach of object detection on-device to instead offer lightweight binary classification architectures suitable for MCUs.
elements.sent-2411-408: Below, we provide further insights from our results, how our method compares with existing “fully-fledged” insect camera traps (meaning those deployed in the field from prior publications) and provide suggestions how others may integrate this method into future designs.
elements.sent-2411-409: We also discuss the limitations of our method and point to some directions for future work.
elements.sent-2411-410: Our results demonstrate that the models are excellent candidates for insect triggers, achieving high accuracy, precision, and F1 scores, with strong specificity and recall across output thresholds, even on AMT-CT which was unseen in training.
elements.sent-2411-411: Saliency map analysis further validated model reliability.
elements.sent-2411-412: Colorblind models relied more often on spurious background information, as shown by a significant drop in $\bar{P _t$.}
elements.sent-2411-413: In contrast, RGB scored higher $\bar{P _t$, boosting confidence in their performance.}
elements.sent-2411-414: Importantly, our approach operates effectively on realistic microcontroller platforms, maintaining low power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-415: Overall, our methodology delivers on each of the aspects of a successful camera trap trigger identified.
elements.sent-2411-416: Although this work does not provide a fully-fledged insect monitoring system, we compare the characteristics of our hardware test with those developed by others in Table \ref{tab:look-at-me .}
elements.sent-2411-417: This reveals several advantages: (1) the power consumption is low enough to facilitate multiple-week deployments given a moderate battery size of 110-Wh, this is far longer than existing systems; (2) We showcase a new hardware platform and AI methodology that has not yet been used in insect monitoring; (3) The framerate achieved by our model execution is greater than the only other MCU-based hardware in the list (\citep{darras , row 2) and considerably lower framerates than devices deployed with more capable computing hardware.}
elements.sent-2411-418: Overall, this niche potentiates large power consumption benefits at lower framerates.

elements.sent-2411-419: Advancements in low-power AI and microcontrollers are rapidly expanding the potential for scalable biodiversity monitoring.

elements.sent-2411-449: Using the latest innovations in MCU hardware and ultra-lightweight supervised learning, we have shown how insect monitoring can be made scalable by addressing the challenging trigger problem.
